data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Recovery Might Take Longer, But Contrarians Know What To Do
data.description:
Introduction

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) is a company that has become closely watched by many investors, hedge funds, and analysts. I have covered the stock three times already. Since my first rating (which was a buy), the stock is down 1% while the market (SPX) is up 17%, which is a very poor performance. At the point of my Strong Buy rating, UNH had dropped further, leading to a slight outperformance since then. My latest Buy rating resulted in a performance of -20%. It is, therefore, reasonable to say that the turnaround thesis betting on UNH's strong history might take a little longer to play out.

This is, in part, due to yesterday's news that Medicare Advantage rates could increase just 0.09% when multiple percentage points were expected. This directly pressures UNH's margins, which is the least what current investors like to see. The company reported their Q4 2025 earnings today, and the stock fell 19% together with yesterday's after-market plunge. Let's dive in.

Financials

Revenues grew 12% YoY in Q4 and are flat QoQ. For the year ended, revenues also grew 12%, but revenue growth was expected. It still missed estimates by $515M, while adjusted EPS of $2.11 were in line. Full year EPS of $16.35 outperformed the firm's own guidance of $16.

A headwind of $1.6B, consisting of final cyberattack costs, net portfolio divestitures, and restructuring costs, hit 2025's bottom line, which could drop moving forward and become a tailwind for margins.

For 2026, the company expects revenues of at least $439B, which implies a decline of 2%. This is unusual, as despite dropping profitability, UNH has always grown revenues (in the last four decades). In my view, this is a sign of healthy restructuring and sawing off a lot of loss-inducing revenue sources, which should be seen as a positive.

At the same time, operating income will come in above $24B at a margin of 5.5%, which points towards a large improvement from last year, no matter whether we're looking at $19B GAAP operating income or $21.7B as adjusted. To be able to achieve higher profits despite lower or flat revenues shows clear signs of successful cost controls and restructuring measures taking effect.

In fact, cost reductions are real and driven by AI integration, as AI tools now handle 80% of member calls.

Management noted during the call that efficiency gains from automation and AI are being rolled out across claims and service functions to both reduce costs and improve turnaround times and member satisfaction.

“We anticipate operating cost reductions of nearly $1 billion in 2026, many AI-enabled and importantly, in higher customer experience and satisfaction at a lower cost.” - CEO Stephen Hemsley.

EPS growth is expected to be just 8.6% from the already depressed 2025 figures, reaching $17.75 as adjusted. This is likely the reason why the stock is down so much and further portrays why the recovery might take a bit longer than expected.

Long-term View

Altogether, there are not a lot of positive things one can mention that UNH has going for them at the moment. At this point, investors have to ask themselves whether they are willing to stay invested given all this uncertainty.

However, times of uncertainty are often the best buying opportunities, as in other scenarios involving more positive sentiment, a lot of future growth is already priced in, increasing the potential downside. At the same time, UNH stock is the same stock. The outlook worsened and has introduced many question marks, but by buying now, you are certainly better off than people who bought at $500-$600 less than a year ago. Because there will be a price UNH trades at in the future, say 2030, and that price will be reached. The lower your cost basis, the better your return is going to be on that price.

This is an important rephrasing. Just basing an investment decision off this mentality would yield terrible results, however, as people would buy companies that are financially unstable, are generally very risky, and have already dropped a ton. The contrarian perspective, therefore, lies in finding a path towards future fundamentals improving again and deeming that path probable enough to invest today. In that case, outsized gains may be made.

So what does this case look like?

First, cash flow is still positive, giving the company room for flexibility. 2026 cash from operations will be around $18B (as per management's guidance), which more than covers their dividends, share repurchases, and CapEx expenditures. Cash is what matters at the end and shows that the company is not remotely close to being at bankruptcy risk. Instead, they can use this downward period to enhance shareholder returns by leveraging a higher buyback yield and paying out a higher dividend yield as well, both benefiting new investors. This also helps create room for future investments, such as:

The company will invest nearly $1.5 billion in technology and AI initiatives in 2026, with a similar level targeted for 2027.

With their new 2026 EPS target, UNH now trades at a forward P/E of 15.8. This is assuming that they reach this target on the dot and do not overshoot, like they did this year. A P/E near 15 does not price in a lot of (if any) future growth, despite the company claiming to be able to return to previous margin levels in 2027. This creates a significant re-rating opportunity.

On the earnings call, management reiterated that the company still views a long-term adjusted EPS growth rate in the range of 13% to 16% as achievable, which is the same growth framework UnitedHealth has historically operated under. At today’s valuation, the market is clearly not pricing in anything close to that outcome. If we assume earnings compound at the lower end of that range and apply a modest valuation normalization of roughly 10% per year for a few years, the implied annualized return lands around 25%.

Risks

Medical costs are hard to forecast and could grow, stay flat, or even decline. UNH is very sensitive to this volatility, as these are their primary costs, and their margins are small, even at historical levels, making small fluctuations have large impacts. This could be a risk and an opportunity at the same time, but due to inflation remaining positive, I believe a rise is more likely than a fall.

Further, funding seems to remain uncertain. Regarding the Medicare Advantage news:

Looking briefly to 2027, the advance notice published yesterday simply doesn’t reflect the reality of medical utilization and cost trends. (...) We will continue to work with CMS to ensure an appropriate final growth rate calculation to avoid a profoundly negative impact on seniors’ benefits and access to care. - Tim Noel.

This shows that these trends are not fully baked into UNH's outlook and could make them miss on their guidance.

Conclusion

In my view, UNH is still a contrarian Buy given all aspects outlined above, aided by its recent selloff. The turnaround might take longer than expected but is still one worth betting on.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: enGene: Two Major Catalysts On Deck For 2nd Half Of 2026
data.description:
The last time I spoke about enGene Holdings (ENGN) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "enGene Holdings: Mid-2024 NMIBC Data Could Lead To EG-70 BLA Filing". As an update, the company is nearing the finish line but not quite there yet. The reason why I state this is because it had gained alignment with the FDA that its pivotal Cohort 1 of the phase 2 LEGEND trial could be used as the basis to be able to file a Biologics Licensing Application [BLA] of its gene therapy detalimogene for the treatment of patients with BCG-unresponsive, high-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer [high-risk NMIBC] with carcinoma in situ [CIS] - with or without papillary disease. The company in November of 2025 released preliminary data from the pivotal cohort post-amendment changes showcasing that the drug was able to achieve a complete response [CR] rate of 62% at 6 months.

If this has already been revealed, then why do I believe that there is value still left here? It is because there are two major catalysts or inflection points that are supposed to happen in the 2nd half of 2026. The first catalyst of which, is that the company intends to release data from pivotal Cohort 1 of this phase 2 LEGEND study of 125 patients with BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS, in the 2nd half of 2026. Barring a positive outcome from this study, this sets up enGene to file its BLA of detalimogene as a gene therapy to treat this type of NMIBC patients.

The most crucial item being that the company believes that this non-viral gene therapy could become a first-line treatment option for these BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC patients. This would be on the basis for ease of use for starters, along with a potentially best-in-class tolerability profile. In my prior article, I rated this company's stock as a "Buy" rating, and this time around I'm maintaining this rating. The reason why is because of the positive preliminary data from the amended pivotal cohort [post-amendment], plus the fact that there could be two major catalysts to come about in the 2nd half of 2026. As with all biotechs there are inherent risks to consider, therefore its a high-risk/high-reward scenario.

Pivotal Cohort On Track For 2nd Half Of 2026 Data Release With Preliminary Data On Hand

As I noted above, the company is in the process of running the phase 2 LEGEND study to potentially use its gene therapy detalimogene for the treatment of patients with BCG-unresponsive, high-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer [NMIBC] with carcinoma in situ [CIS]. Before going over this mid-stage trial, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, it is first important to go over what this type of cancer is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Bladder cancer [BC] is characterized as the growth of malignant cells on the lining of the bladder. Specifically, the urothelial cells that line the bladder. There are two sides of the coin with bladder cancer. The good news is that it is typically caught very early on, which means that it is highly treatable. The bad news [flip side] is that despite being treatable early on, there is a high recurrence rate [cancer coming back after treatment]. Another fact to state is that urothelial cells are found in the kidney and tubes of the kidneys as well. While cancer can occur in these structures, bladder cancer tends to be more common in the bladder of a patient. Then, it's important to note what the term "non-muscle invasive" bladder cancer means. It means that the bladder cancer has formed on the inside lining but has not spread directly into the muscle of the bladder itself. When this actually does happen, this is known as muscle-invasive bladder cancer [MIBC]. The company is focused on NMIBC, and there are more patients here, as follows:

  • Non-muscle invasive bladder cancer [NMIBC] - 61,000 new cases per year in the United States
  • Muscle-invasive bladder cancer [MIBC] - 20,000 new cases per year in the United States

There is a therapy known as BCG or Bacillus Calmette-Guérin [BCG] , which is used for early-stage disease. The hope is that either BCG or another therapy prevents the cancer from coming back. However, ultimately, the last available option is a radical cystectomy. What the hope here is that detalimogene can prevent the recurrence of disease and allow such BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC patients to avoid the need for such a radical surgery option. The basis of being a bad option is not just because it is surgery, but in that it does carry major problems [enGene Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #6] They are as follows:

  • 6% to 15% mortality rate
  • Decreased quality of life [QOL] because of needing a stoma bag and also having sexual dysfunction as well

How does it work? It uses its Dually Derivatized Oligochitosan or DDX platform to be instilled in the bladder to specially only generate a local tumor immune response. The reason for this is to avoid any system-wide response that would otherwise contribute to systemic toxicity. The beauty of detalimogene is that it carries other competitive advantages as well such as:

  • Delivery tech helped overcome problem with viral gene therapies
  • No need to safe handling and cold storage
  • Simpler manufacturing process

Thereby, this therapy has all of these advantages, plus potentially being a first-line option for these BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS patients.

As I noted above, the company is looking at moving forward with the pivotal cohort of the phase 2 LEGEND trial, which is Cohort 1. This specific cohort intends to evaluate 125 of these BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS patients. The primary endpoint that is deployed is anytime complete response [CR] rate. The 125 patients are split up into two sections which is a pre-protocol change and post-protocol change as follows [enGene Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #16]:

  • 94 post-protocol amendment patients [64 patients with one post-baseline scan with data I'm going to go over below] - 6 month CR rate of 62%
  • 31 pre-protocol amendment patients - 6 month CR rate of 41%

Where a risk factor pours in, though, is that the final [N] of patients is to be determined based on a statistical analysis plan [SAP] with the FDA []. Thus, it remains to be seen what the final outcome for the amended protocol is. The CR rate at any time, though, was said to be 63%. The good news is that there are expansion opportunities for this program. This includes targeting other NMIBC patients, like those who are BCG-naive high-risk ones and a few others. This brings forth the two milestones or catalysts I noted above. The first of which is that the company believes that it is on track to release Cohort 1 data from the phase 2 study of detalimogene for the treatment of these patients with BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC in the 2nd half of 2026. Barring a positive outcome, along with alignment for an SAP with the FDA on the [N] of patients to be evaluated, it should be able to file its BLA of this gene therapy in the 2nd half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, enGEne Holdings had $202.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities as of October 31, 2025. The thing is that the cash on hand that it had wasn't going to be enough to fund its operations for an extended period of time at all. However, that's why in November of 2025 it enact a successful underwritten public offering of its common stock and pre-funded warrants at an offering price of $8.5 per share and $8.4999 per pre-funded warrant. The total from this offering that was generated was $140.1 million in net proceeds. The estimate was that the company would be able to fund its operations into the 2nd half of 2028 with this newly founded cash. Despite the cash on hand, plus additional monies raised, it still chose to enter into an amended and restated loan with Hercules Capital. This would be for a total of $125 million as a debt facility to help fund the ongoing late-stage clinical study of detalimogene and to help fund the commercialization efforts as well.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in enGene Holdings. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the expected release of data from Cohort 1 of the ongoing phase 2 LEGEND study using detalimogene to treat 125 BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS. First and foremost, the amended protocol with the FDA changed the primary endpoint for this trial to be complete response [CR] at any time. With that being said, the CR at any time for the 62 patients under the amended protocol had CR at any time of 63%. The risk here is that even though this interim analysis brought such a CR number, there is no assurance this will be carried over upon the release of final data from this trial.

The second catalyst has to do with a potential BLA filing of detalimogene to the FDA for the treatment of BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS. This submission is expected to happen in the latter half of 2026. Of course, that's only if the pivotal cohort from the phase 2 LEGEND study achieves its intended primary efficacy endpoint. Even if a BLA is filed with the FDA, there is a risk that the submission may not end up being accepted in its present form for starters. If the FDA does decide to accept the submission for review of detalimogene for the treatment of these patients, there is no assurance that enGene will be granted U.S. marketing approval for it.

The third and final risk would be several expansion opportunities. The basis here is that the company is not only in the process of evaluating detalimogene for the treatment of BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC patients. It has three other cohorts, and the status of these is as follows:

  • BCG-naive high-risk NMIBC with CIS patients under cohort 2a - 30 patients enrolled in study
  • BCG-exposed high-risk, NMIBC with CIS patients under cohort 2b - 45 patients enrolled in study
  • BCG-unresponsive high-risk, papillary-only NMIBC - 36 patients enrolled in study

The main point that I'm trying to get at here is that even though detalimogene has been shown to work well in BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS, there is no assurance that it will work in one or more of these other expansion opportunities. In this case, the company would have to rely on its main program only.

Conclusion

The thing is that enGene has done well for itself. It established alignment with the FDA that it could amend the protocol in Cohort 1 of the phase 2 LEGEND study. This study is evaluating the use of detalimogene for the treatment of patients with BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS - with or without papillary disease. There are the two major infection points that I went over above, which, if achieved in a positive manner, should yield a higher stock price because of it. Specifically, the interim analysis brought forth any time CR rate of 63% in post-amendment patients who had completed at least one post baseline disease assessment. With the release of final data from Cohort 1 of the phase 2 LEGEND study using detalimogene for BCG-unresponsive, high-risk NMIBC with CIS patients in the 2nd half of 2026, plus a potential BLA filing at the same time period [should the primary endpoint be met in a statistically significant manner], I believe that this stock should be maintained with a "Buy" Rating.

data.title: Gold Above $5,000/Oz? 2 Bullish Factors For PHYS, 1 That Worries Me (A Lot)
data.description:
2025 was a “golden” year for CEFs such as Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS).

Which of you readers, even just for a moment, thought: “it has grown too much from …”? Let’s be honest, judging only from the chart, few would have expected such a sparkling start to 2026. Instead, a new record: $5,000/oz.

In the end, price matters little for assets driven by supply and demand like gold: what matters are the drivers that guide these two components. And the question therefore arises naturally: “which ones are still today playing in favor of gold, therefore of PHYS?” And above all, “which ones will continue to do so?” Or conversely, “which ones will interrupt this trend?”

Personally, I identify 2 positive drivers, and one that, to tell the truth, has disturbed my serenity.

But before going deeper into it …

What to know about PHYS

PHYS is a Closed-End Trust, or CEF, with a value of $18.7 billion, one of the largest physical gold-backed non-ETF vehicles. It was created with the objective of physically holding gold in London Good Delivery bars, 100% physical gold, not derivatives, fully allocated and unencumbered: this entails no counterparty risk related to gold leasing. The redemption mechanism is physical or cash, and the gold is held at the Royal Canadian Mint.

The MER is 0.39% while the expense ratio is 0.41%. This is sufficiently covered by the fact that PHYS’ NAV is still trading at a -1.77% discount. A discount that is purely quantitative, not qualitative: there is no “economic” dilution of the backing. On the contrary, fluctuations over the 2014-2026 period have progressively narrowed between -3% and +2%. No anomalies are in fact highlighted by the quant rating, or by the Analyst Breakdown of SA.

Peer

PHYS grows less than its ETF peers, but personally I do not see this as a problem: the underperformance is justified by the more pronounced costs typical of the CEF structure. In this sense, a comparison with ETF solutions can be useful: for simplicity, I will limit myself to reporting the two with the largest AUM, SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD) and iShares Gold Trust ETF (IAU).

From the expense ratio perspective, PHYS does not cost much more than GLD and personally, I consider the difference with IAU not that significant. Not by chance, the 5Y performance diverges by just a few percentage points on a variation of more than 160%.

What I consider positive in this regard for PHYS is the fact that today there is still a discount above 1.5%, which could potentially alleviate the burden of a higher expense ratio.

The drivers of gold growth

I am not saying anything new when I say that the number one driver that has pushed gold prices higher in recent years has been central bank purchases. And if it is true that price depends on supply and demand, a first question to ask is: “will this demand-side propulsion continue in 2026?” Spoiler: neither yes nor no.

Demand

According to JPMorgan’s forecasts, it makes sense to expect average demand of ~585 tonnes per quarter, which would explain about 70% of the variation in gold prices. Not bad, considering that on average every +100 tonnes above 350 corresponds to roughly a +2% q/q. Most of this will come from central banks: a projected flow of 755 tonnes in 2026, lower than the peaks above 1,000 tonnes, but still well above the pre-2022 average (400–500). Alongside this are private flows: today gold represents only 2.8% of global AUM; this share is expected to expand in 2026, thanks to inflows of around +250 tonnes. Still less than half of the flows seen in previous bull markets. Therefore, on the demand side, personally I do not see structural issues that could interfere with growth.

Macro

If we think about it, gold has grown in a fairly adverse macro environment: the U.S. dollar (DXY) and the US 10-year TIPS real yield are at cyclically elevated levels. The drivers of growth in this case have been U.S. debt > $38.5T (positive correlation between deficits and gold prices), but above all, geopolitical pressures (sanctions, tariffs, and then the recent geopolitical fragmentation). Question: “sorry, but hasn’t the dollar actually gone down?” Yes, but in absolute terms we are still in the upper range.

Question 2: “sorry, but isn’t the Fed cutting rates?” In my view, even here, it is not entirely correct to say that Fed cuts have changed monetary conditions to the detriment of gold: in fact, these have not lowered yields because longer maturities continue to carry positive real premiums.

Considering that geopolitical pressures are ongoing, the debt problem is still present, and both the dollar and yields can still exert downward pressure, even from a macro perspective, I do not see elements that could interfere with growth.

Risk

JPMorgan’s targets, which incorporated (as I personally interpreted them) the demand drivers, were ~$5,000/oz for Q4 2026 and ~$5,400/oz for the end of 2027. Let’s say that the price of gold reached the first target a bit earlier than expected …

So implicitly, does the price of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) already price in the positive drivers for 2026? A legitimate question in my view. Well potentially yes … which moves away from the idea that in the short term gold could have further short-term propulsive pushes like the one at the beginning of 2026.

And in what way could this “haste” in rising penalize gold? I read it differently: if prices rise, fewer tonnes are needed to satisfy institutional purchases, therefore the probability of congestion increases.

Conclusion

Therefore, on gold I see 2 positive drivers for 2026, the one on the demand side (technical), and the macroeconomic side. Two drivers that, however, seem to have been partially already priced in by the market amid the speculative mania of recent days. I still believe that this does not undermine the positive market sentiment, and in any case justifies a Buy rating.

data.title: 'Stayin' Alive' In 2026 With The Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund
data.description:
"Stayin' Alive" Means "Muddling Through" Uncertain Times

Some of us remember the movie "Saturday Night Fever" where the title song "Stayin' Alive" captured the day-to-day challenges facing the lead character, Tony Manero, played so convincingly by John Travolta.

I see "Stayin' Alive" as an appropriate goal for my investing generally, but especially in 2026, given the unprecedented risks and unknowns we face in our economy, our country overall, and in our global geopolitical environment.

Investors familiar with my Income Factory® philosophy and strategy know that I strive to achieve what has been the long-term average equity total return of 9-10% per annum by relying on high cash yields, so I'm not dependent on constant capital gains like typical "equity growth" strategies. By contrast, since the S&P 500 only pays an average dividend yield of 1.5%, an equity investor needs additional capital gains averaging 7% or 8% every year in order to meet that 9-10% total return target.

Both strategies can get you to the same place several decades later, but many of us find an Income Factory strategy is easier to stick to through "thick and thin" when we're still collecting, reinvesting, and compounding an 8-9% (or more) "river of cash," even when the price of our portfolio may be dropping. Compare that to the additional stress on an equity investor during down years, where their capital is dropping in value and they're only collecting a 1.5% "trickle of cash."

Cohen & Stearns Closed End Opportunity Fund (FOF), is a solid, steady-Eddie fund that has been cranking out a high cash distribution (plus or minus 8% yields) for almost 20 years while managing to maintain its net asset value and market price along the way. At the risk of giving away the rest of this article's storyline, I believe FOF is the kind of durable, "non-heroic" investment that should be able to thrive if markets do well this year. But if markets do not do well, it should be able, at least, to successfully cope with whatever volatility and challenges we may encounter.

Finding A "Non-Heroic" Investment For All Seasons

"Non-heroic" is the term I use to describe investments where all the issuer and its management have to do is "muddle through" whatever adversity they encounter in their specific markets, or in the broader economic and political environment, however uncertain. For credit investments, like loans, bonds, and other fixed income asset classes, "muddle through" means issuers don't have to do anything except stay alive and pay their debts. For conservative, relatively stable, cash flow oriented equity investments, like utilities, infrastructure, or real estate companies, my "muddling through" expectation is that they'll carry on with "business as usual," continue to pay dividends at current or close to current levels, but with no earnings growth anticipated or required.

The common theme is that to meet my long-term goal of a steady, growing cash income, all I need is for my investment portfolio, as a diversified whole, to stay the course and do what it's currently doing. It's like going to a racetrack and being able to bet on horses to merely "finish the race" and make it around the track. They don't have to win or even finish near the front of the pack. It's a much easier bet to win than buying equity, which is more like betting on horses to win, place, or show (i.e. do better than average, grow their business, etc.).

Given our country's current challenges and uncertainties, if my investments can just do that in 2026 - keep their heads down and survive, avoiding major obstacles or mistakes - while continuing to pay me most or all of my current distribution stream, I'll be a happy camper.

Besides not wanting my investments to be "heroes" that have to outperform their peers or the rest of the market, I also want to be sure there are lots of them, so if some of them falter, there are plenty of others to carry the load. In other words, I want sufficient diversification, so I don't have too many investment eggs in one basket.

Wait a minute! You Want One Security To Do All That?

It would be unrealistic to try to utilize a single security to provide all the safety, security, and diversification we want in our total portfolio. But if I had to pick one single investment to start with, in a year with all the unknowns and potential volatility we face in 2026, my top choice would be Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund. FOF, as its symbol suggests, is a true "fund of funds." With more than 100 separate funds in its portfolio, each one highly diversified with dozens and/or hundreds of its own investments, FOF provides substantial diversification by industry and individual issuer, all within a single fund.

FOF was started in 2006, and its stated investment objective is "to seek to achieve total return consisting of high current income and potential capital appreciation through investments in the common stock of closed-end management investment companies that invest significantly in equity or income-producing securities."

FOF has managed to do that pretty well, with total returns, as shown below, averaging 12.7% (market price) and 11% (NAV) per annum over the past 10 years, and 13.3% and 20.4%, respectively, over the past year. The fact that the market price return was less than the NAV return this past year reflects how the price has dropped from a premium of over 5% back to a slight discount in the past six months. That means FOF's a better value now than it was last summer.

Note: For readers less familiar with closed-end funds, please note that a closed-end fund's total return is expressed two ways. "Total return (market price)" equals the sum of the fund's cash distribution plus the change (i.e., capital gain or loss) in its market price from the beginning to the end of the reporting period. That's how a typical stock's total return is calculated. A closed-end fund's "total return (NAV)" is also reported, and equals its cash distribution plus the change in its net asset value ("NAV") over the course of the accounting period. The total return (NAV) is how traditional "open end" mutual funds report their results, since they're usually bought and sold directly with the fund sponsor/manager (i.e., Fidelity, Schwab, Vanguard, etc.), which sells and redeems the fund each day at its most recent closing NAV, which is the sum of the market values of all the assets the fund holds, divided by the number of shares it has outstanding. Closed-end fund shares are bought and sold in public stock markets, so their market price can vary, sometimes substantially, from their NAV. That variation is reported as a premium or discount, and it allows patient, careful investors an opportunity to time their purchases and often get bargain prices where a fund is selling for less than the sum of the market prices of the assets it holds.

FOF has paid a consistent $1.04 per year in distributions since 2010 (as a quarterly payout 2010-2016, and as a monthly since 2016), which is currently a 7.7% yield. That's a nice combination of total return (i.e., 10% or more for the past 10 years) and cash yield (plus or minus 8%), since it means the difference between the two represents the amount of total return that stays in the fund after it pays its distribution and is able to sustain and boost its net asset value over time.

This is important to long-term income investors who want to collect a generous distribution yield but also want to be sure their longer-term principal value stays intact (or grows) as well and that the fund isn't "eating its seed corn" by paying out in distributions more than it actually earns.

The above chart shows FOF's market price return alone, after excluding the dividend. It shows how, despite all the price volatility during the past five years, it has managed to maintain and even grow its market value over the course of that period, after making its consistent, high yield distributions.

This is a good example of how a steady, high-yield "reinvest and compound" income investment program (whether we call it an "Income Factory" or not) can work for us. Remember that during the periods when FOF's price was way down on this chart, its dividend was still the same solid $.87 per month (or its quarterly equivalent) that it's been since 2010.

So the yield and reinvestment rate were higher than ever during those down periods. This is why we say that when prices drop, it allows us to grow our income streams even faster, as we reinvest in additional earning assets (more "machines" for our Income Factory) at bargain prices and yields.

For example, with total returns like FOF's, of 10% and higher, an investor's capital would double, and then redouble again, and again, every seven years, if they reinvested and compounded their dividends, like most of us when investing for future retirement. That means after 14 years it would be 4 times its original capital, 8 times after 21 years, 16 times after 28 years, 32 times after 35 years, etc. And that doesn't even include the considerable impact of making additional annual investments along the way.

This is what steady, predictable "income investing," can do for us over the long term. Not aiming to "beat the market," but merely letting consistent cash yields and compound interest (the "8th wonder of the world" per Albert Einstein) do its thing.

But it gets even better. During those down years, FOF often sold at more of a discount to its NAV, and since it was buying other funds that were themselves often selling at discounts, you got the "discount on discount" advantage described in more detail down below.

Lots of Diversification in a Single Fund

As described earlier, even as a single fund, FOF provides considerable diversification. FOF itself owns more than 100 separate funds, so there's substantial diversification by asset class and by manager. A wide range of equity funds account for 54% of the portfolio, and commodities (21%) and fixed income (19%) comprise most of the rest. Within each of those categories, there's additional diversification, with each separate fund investing in dozens or hundreds of separate issuers itself.

Source: FOF website

One final feature is the opportunity to take advantage of discounts when you're buying closed-end funds. This is an important advantage for FOF, since almost all of its holdings are closed-end funds, although it dabbles in a few other ETFs and specialized funds as well. At times in the past, FOF has sold at a considerable discount itself, which meant the opportunity to get "discounts on discounts" (sort of like "double coupons" at the grocery store) was an important attraction of the fund. I wrote about this in one of my first articles on FOF more than a decade ago. More recently, as prices of funds, including FOF, have risen, many of the discounts are less generous. But based on the average discounts on FOF's 10 largest holdings, which account for 46% of its total portfolio, FOF shareholders are still enjoying slightly more than a 3% price discount on the shares of the underlying funds that FOF holds, when they buy them via an investment in FOF.

Pulling It All Together

FOF is a really solid fund that has been paying attractive, steady distributions for many years, from a highly diversified, actively managed portfolio of mostly closed-end funds. Many of the funds FOF holds sell at discounts, from which FOF shareholders get the same benefits (i.e. having more assets working for them than they actually had to pay for) that they would if they had bought those discounted funds directly.

In the past (including the "discount on discounts" article referenced above), I have described FOF as sort of an "instant Income Factory" because of its wide diversification and intensive focus on paying out a consistent and generous cash distribution.

In a year like this one, when "all heck could break loose" (but hopefully won't) in the financial, economic, or geopolitical arenas, if I had to select a single fund that is poised to benefit if the markets do well, but also to provide considerable shelter from the elements if things go south, it would be Cohen & Steers' FOF.

As always, I welcome your comments and questions.

data.title: Silver Prices: What's Driving The Rally And What Could Reverse It
data.description:
Silver prices have been defying gravity of late. Net buying by silver investors has been the key factor driving silver prices up sharply. While silver prices have been in an upward trend since early 2024, the rally accelerated during the second half of 2025. Prices could still rise higher, but greater price volatility should be expected going forward.

What Keeps The Party Going

There is a long litany of political, macroeconomic, financial market, and silver market supply and demand factors that are helping to drive silver prices higher.

Political risk, and the use of silver as a portfolio diversifier to hedge this risk, is expected to be the key factor keeping net silver investment demand elevated in the medium term. Political risk tends to increase demand for assets like gold and silver, which have no counterparty risk, among investors looking to diversify away from sovereign exposure. Political risk includes both domestic as well as cross border risks.

For some time now, there has been a general deterioration in political relations between various countries around the world. This deterioration in relations has intensified recently.

Political risks have numerous and serious consequences on economic conditions. One outcome of this deterioration of relations is the acceleration in deglobalization, which has a two-fold negative impact. One is on long-term global economic growth, and the second is on the ability of countries to recover from a global economic downturn.

Actions like those witnessed between the United States and Venezuela may serve as rhetorical cover for Russia and China to legitimize their respective actions in Ukraine and Taiwan, potentially accelerating the normalization of territorial expansion via military force. Such erosion of global norms materially raises international political risks.

The market has also grown concerned about the Fed’s independence. An attack on the Fed’s independence is by extension an attack on the credibility of the U.S. dollar. Growing political pressure on Federal Reserve independence, alongside widening fiscal deficits and recurring government shutdown risks, suggests sustained upward pressure on U.S. Treasury yields. Elevated yields would further weigh on economic growth by raising borrowing costs for households, businesses, and the public sector.

Among macroeconomic factors, there is the K-shaped economy and concerns about inflation and affordability. While the concept of a K-shaped economy is nothing new, it has taken on a greater level of importance due to its infiltration in developed countries. The dependence on a small cohort of the population to support entire economies creates a precarious economic situation. The K-shaped economy also creates persistent political and policy stress, increasing the chances of a political misstep or policy error that threatens various risks, ranging from recessions to social instability.

It is also important to distinguish between the rate of inflation and absolute price levels. While the rate of inflation has come down sharply, the price levels still are significantly higher than their pre-pandemic levels, creating an affordability issue. It is also worth noting that much of the affordability issue has occurred on non-discretionary items, putting further pressure on the lower arm of the K-shaped economy.

Financial markets, particularly the stock markets, have risen strongly in recent years. While investors remain optimistic about the future of stock markets, there is a growing unease about the sustainability of this strength. Arguments can be made for both a correction as well as continued strength in equity markets. The uncertainty of the direction gives investors further reason to hold a portfolio diversifier like silver.

As mentioned before, there are a lot of reasons why investors are flocking to silver. I have covered some of these above, but there are many more. The point is, investors have a lot of reasons to own alternative assets like silver as a portfolio diversifier at times like the present. It is also important to understand that conditions are likely to get worse before they get better, and this has been giving investors an added nudge in the direction of assets like gold and silver.

In addition to political and macroeconomic risks, investors also have reason to add silver to their portfolios for silver’s own supply and fabrication demand fundamentals.

On the fabrication demand side, the metal has uses in numerous important industries, ranging from artificial intelligence to solar panels. On the supply side, mine production is primarily a byproduct of gold or base metals. The lackluster performance of silver prices for an extended period of time reduced the amount of investment made toward building primary silver mines. If silver prices can sustain themselves at elevated levels, which is the base case scenario, investment toward mining will rise. But it takes years for mines to be brought onstream.

What Takes The Punch Bowl Away

The high price of silver comes with its own risks. One of the areas most sensitive to these high prices is fabrication demand. While there is a lot of focus on the use of silver in various electronic applications, the largest fabricated use of silver continues to be silver jewelry. Jewelry demand accounts for around 30% of global silver fabrication demand. Demand for silver jewelry is very sensitive to prices. Because jewelry demand is discretionary in nature, the high price of the metal typically tends to discourage its demand. Additionally, at a time like the present, where discretionary items are taking up larger portions of people’s incomes, items like jewelry fall further down on the list.

That said, the higher silver price does not only negatively impact jewelry demand but also demand from other sources of fabrication. Fabricators are always trying to reduce input costs to the extent possible without compromising quality and efficiency. The recent surge in prices will accelerate efforts to reduce or, if possible, substitute silver in a variety of fabricated uses. While these changes can take some time to be commercialized, there are already past efforts to reduce and substitute silver in various fabricated products becoming commercially available. One such development is the commercialization of silver-free solar panels, with two major Chinese solar panel manufacturers announcing the move.

In addition to the impact of silver prices on fabrication demand, overall demand from various industries also goes through cycles. The auto industry, for example, which is a major user of silver, is expected to experience weakness during 2026.

Another factor that is very sensitive to prices is secondary supply. The surge in silver prices will bring back into the market old, broken jewelry or other silver-containing items that consumers around the world hold. The high price also increases the incentive for recyclers to process items with low silver volumes per unit. These developments are expected to drive silver scrap supply higher, which could act as a headwind to prices. Silver scrap supply accounts for.

There also are several short-term tactical traders that have entered the silver market, chasing the silver price momentum higher. If silver prices stall or soften, these momentum-driven traders could be seen exiting the market further, compounding the decline.

Conclusion

Silver prices are expected to remain at historically elevated levels, with further gains in prices possible.

The desire among investors to hold portfolio diversifier in the current political and economic environment is expected to be the primary driver for silver investment demand and prices. Silver price volatility should be expected to rise, going forward.

Over the longer term, changes to the silver market fundamentals due to current high prices could cause silver prices to come down from their lofty levels. High prices will eventually result in an increase in supply both from mines as well as scrap and will permanently destroy the use of silver in certain fabricated products.

While these changes to supply and fabrication demand will bring silver prices down in the future, silver prices should be expected to remain at levels higher than they have been over much of the past. This permanent upward shift in the silver price floor can be expected from the ongoing political risks to the global economy, which will result in a more strategic allocation of silver in investor portfolios.

data.title: CoreWeave: Nvidia Signals AI Story Is Still Early
data.description:
With Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) doubling down on investing in CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), the market can relax on the worries regarding debt and AI demand. The AI cloud provider is in the midst of a massive AI data center buildout, and the recent stock move is disconnected from the market opportunity. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on CoreWeave around $100.

Nvidia Investment

Nvidia agreed to invest another $2 billion in CoreWeave at $87.20 per share. The chip company initially owned 24.3 million shares for a 6.2% position in CoreWeave, but the company had 498 million shares outstanding in Q3 '25 for a position below 5%.

The deal announcement is interesting in the statement regarding accelerating the ability of CoreWeave to build out 5 gigawatts of AI factories by 2030. The AI cloud company had ~590 MW of active power at the end of Q3 and had contracts to reach ~2.9 GW of power within the next 24 months.

The Nvidia deal appears to suggest CoreWeave plans to add another 5 GW of power for AI factories by 2030 to reach a total of 7.9 GW. Following the termination of the Core Scientific (CORZ) deal, CoreWeave has pushed towards developing data centers internally versus leasing from unreliable suppliers.

The important story here is that CoreWeave is going from only having 590 MW of connected power in Q3, up 120 MW sequentially from Q2. The company forecast Q4 sales at $1.54 billion for just over $5 billion for the year.

Due to the focus on AI cloud services and leading GPUs as a service, CoreWeave is on pace to produce somewhere around $1 billion in revenue for each 100 MW of connected power. CoreWeave still has to spend a substantial amount of capital to go from the 590 MW of power to 2.9 GW, but the company will produce substantial revenue growth as well.

The company forecasts spending ~$7 billion in Q4 capex to reach upwards of $14 billion for 2025, with the initial 2026 forecast to more than double capex spending to an amount at least topping $25 billion. The Nvidia investment is only a small part of the story, but the AI chip giant isn't likely to throw another $2 billion at CoreWeave if the company doesn't have the capital and demand lined up to complete this buildout to reach the point of strong cash flows.

Relative Value

The stock jumped back above $100 due to the vote of confidence by Nvidia, but CoreWeave traded to $180 following the hot IPO. Other AI cloud providers like IREN (IREN) and Nebius (NBIS) have surged in the last few months and actually trade close to all-time highs.

IREN now trades at over 6x forward one-year sales, while CoreWeave is a relative value down at only 2.5x sales.

While this simplistic view does miss the component of the future capital position, all of these AI data center plays have to raise capital to build data center capacity and/or acquire Nvidia GPUs. In the case of CoreWeave, the company already has over $20 billion in net PP&E with net debt at only $10 billion.

Even before this Nvidia deal, CoreWeave was forecast to produce 135% revenue growth in 2026 to $12.1 billion and another 62% growth in 2027 to $19.6 billion. Though the revenue numbers only amount to about 1.9 GW of power, the Nvidia deal proclaims CoreWeave has plans for another 6 GW of power to lease.

The CoreWeave growth story is just in the early innings, even with the 2027 revenue target of $19 billion. This Nvidia deal brings to light the expanding plans for the company to meet targets.

While the market is focused on the major hyperscaler deals, CoreWeave has tripled the customers with more than $100 million in annual spend. On the Q3 '25 earnings call, the CEO discussed the expanding customer base beyond the 3 major hyperscalers as follows:

Our exceptional growth illustrates just how quickly AI adoption is progressing beyond the frontier AI labs and hyperscalers. Broader global demand and our recent large wins are driving diversification of our revenue base. For example, the number of customers that exceeded $100 million of revenue over the last 12 months tripled year-over-year.

CoreWeave outlined deals with AI companies like Poolside, Periodic Labs, and Jasper, along with working with NASA's Jet Propulsion Lab. As well, the company announced a deal with Runway to support AI video generation models.

Assuming CoreWeave ends 2026 with net debt in the $30 billion range from about $20 billion in debt from capex spending offset by the Nvidia equity investment other other such moves, the stock would have an enterprise value in the $75 billion range. The stock would only trade at 5.5x EV/EBITDA targets while the metric is forecast to grow 68%.

Also, CoreWeave is at the point of producing operating cash flows to help fund some of the funding going forward. The company has limited expenses other than the depreciation from the capex and interest expenses.

The biggest risk to the investment story is that AI demand suddenly slumps or data center development is pushed out. The market could run into issues with CPU servers or memory shortages that push out the opening of new data centers, impacting revenues while CoreWeave is stuck paying heavy interest expenses. A company with large debt balances can't afford a lot of pushouts, not to mention investors are already concerned big hyperscaler customers like Meta Platforms (META) and OpenAI (OPENAI) are committing to too much data center capacity.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that CoreWeave remains beaten down due to the market not appreciating the growth story. The Nvidia investment signals the story is only beginning, and investors are still early.

data.title: Cherry Hill Mortgage: Performance Concerns Lead To Downgrade Of Preferreds
data.description:
Introduction

Cherry Hill Mortgage Investment Corporation (CHMI) is an mREIT, or a real estate investment trust that invests in mortgages. In addition to offering a high dividend yield on its common shares, the company also offers two preferred shares. The Series A preferred shares (CHMI.PR.A) are fixed and currently yielding over 9.5%. The Series B preferred shares (CHMI.PR.B) are floating and currently yielding 10.4%. I wrote about the Series B preferred shares back in March, with a bullish argument. Today, the company’s overall performance has me a bit more concerned; therefore, I am downgrading both preferred shares to a hold.

Cherry Hill Earnings Results

Like many other mREITs, Cherry Hill got into trouble in 2023 after short-term interest rates rose sharply to battle inflation. The company was holding a lot of mortgages underwritten during the pandemic era with low, fixed interest rates against borrowed funds with short-term interest rates.

The result in 2023 turned into a borrowing rate that exceeded the company’s average asset yield. On an interest income and expense basis, the same trend held true. Interest incomes grew, but interest expenses surged. Ultimately, net interest income went negative for five consecutive quarters. Fortunately for Cherry Hill, the Federal Reserve has recently lowered interest rates and allowed for interest spreads and net interest income to improve. Net interest income in the third quarter rose to $3.3 million, the highest level in three years.

One advantage that helped Cherry Hill carry itself through interest rate volatility was mortgage servicing. Mortgage servicing involves taking on the responsibility of ensuring mortgages are paid in a timely fashion in exchange for a small fee. For Cherry Hill, the income generated from that activity, known as net servicing revenue, helped keep the company profitable during that time and continues to contribute to profits today, although net servicing revenue is decreasing.

The Biggest Deterrent: Leverage

One of the factors keeping me away from Cherry Hill currently is the company’s growing leverage. While the company’s investments have grown, it has been fueled heavily by new borrowing. The leverage ratio has gone from a little over 3:1 to over 5:1 in the past four years. Leverage can become a problem in terms of growing earnings or even maintaining earnings if interest rates move against you (by going higher). Leverage also limits the company’s ability to grow its investments.

Most of Cherry Hill’s debt is short-term in nature, maturing in under 60 days. A higher move in interest rates would give the company very little time to position itself against the negative effects. Also, while Cherry Hill does engage in hedging to help with the effects of interest rates, the hedge ratio is considered historically low right now and may not be helpful enough if interest rates increase.

Book Value and Dividend History Don’t Help Either

While many mREITs struggle to grow book value, Cherry Hill’s journey has been especially difficult. Since 2020, book value per share has decreased from over $12 per share to well under $4 per share. While it’s not crashing towards zero, I don’t want to wait until it gets there to realize my preferred shares are impaired.

What makes the situation even worse with respect to book value is the management projections. In the interest rate sensitivity section of the company’s earnings presentation, Cherry Hill management essentially projects book value per share will be flat no matter what happens with near-term changes to interest rates. This is less than inspiring for investors who expect lower interest rates to increase book value and share prices. What’s most shocking to me is the interest rate sensitivity analysis includes a recent 33% reduction in dividend to preserve capital. Even that cut was insufficient to project book value growth.

Conclusion

I’m downgrading Cherry Hill’s preferred shares to a hold based on the book value erosion, stale interest rate sensitivity analysis, and high leverage. I believe that if the company deleverages, that could strengthen the position of the preferred shares and lead to an upgrade, but until then, I believe income investors should not invest new money into the Cherry Hill preferred shares.

data.title: Palantir's Q4 Preview: Show Me Durability
data.description:
Arguably, the narrative embedded in sell-side previews about Palantir's (PLTR) earnings tends to be around Q4 revenue beats, AIP customer adds, or about how robust margins came in. In my previous coverage (November last year), however, I argued that for investors already sitting on meaningful gains, the real risk was neither holding nor trimming PLTR per se—but acting emotionally or failing to recognize a potential regime change.

However, it is not necessarily explicit in this model that PLTR is no longer pricing in quarterly execution in isolation, but rather whether the market believes that PLTR has indeed crossed an institutional adoption threshold that permanently changes its volatility, shareholder base, and multiples regime.

Anticipating that Q4 will be much less about numbers and more about validation and confirmation of a structural transition, I present a cautiously bullish update on Palantir stock.

The Rarely Covered Angle: Palantir as a Volatility Compression Story

Under the current context of Palantir's thesis, the question around its 4Q25 earnings results tends not to be necessarily around how the company can still grow, but rather how much Palantir can no longer fall.

Trading at an eye-watering 234x forward earnings, and 91x forward revenue multiples, when the market looks at PLTR, it has historically noted: (1) revenue still accelerating—63% YoY in 3Q25, mid-point of guidance for 4Q25 pointing to +61% YoY; (2) strong technology narrative; (3) difficult peer comparison; (4) unstable multiples; and (5) overreaction to any marginal data-moves post-earnings—average ~16.4% (8 quarters).

This in itself defines a high-volatility asset (considering implied volatility around ~58-59%), not just in price but in perception.

And volatility here is defined by structural uncertainty about the (1) predictability of revenues-in this case, with timing of RPO recognition at ~48% in the next 12 months, ~40% in 13-36 months, the rest thereafter; (2) durability of AI demand; (3) customer behavior in recession; and (4) pricing power over time.

Looking at this volatility compression story through the lens of a traditional growth equity model seems to be a big mistake. That is, the narrative that "PLTR deserves a higher multiple if it grows faster" seems to be more erroneous than the narrative that "PLTR deserves a more stable multiple because business risk has fallen, even if growth doesn't accelerate."

And with that comes the silent transition to PLTR, from optional to embedded. If before the company was perceived as discretionary CapEx/OpEx, now (potentially), PLTR should start to look like decision-making infrastructure, integrated into operational flows, connected to critical processes, and with a high cost of reversal. And that changes everything. Embedded software doesn't necessarily grow faster, but it stops falling.

When this transition takes place, it tends to compress the volatility of the PLTR. If the market prices (1) expected growth, and (2) dispersion of possible outcomes, the volatility compression tends to come from the second point. If the belief that Palantir's installed base is resilient, contracts are renewed almost automatically, expansions come more from depth than from new logos, and the software is no longer a "pilot" but a "nervous system".

Then, (1) the pessimistic scenario is no longer "growth breaks down"; and (2) it becomes "growth slows down, but doesn't implode". And this ultimately shrinks the left tail of the distribution.

If all this may have sounded "dense", basically the message is that, for Palantir, 4Q25 is much more about the disappearance of the downside than any possible extra upside, in my opinion.

Why Palantir’s Q4 Matters Less for Growth—and More for Durability

The market already knows very well how strong Palantir can execute. As a result, the net result is for short-term beats to no longer change history, just as small misses don't invalidate the robustness of multi-year contracts.

So what really matters is whether the speeches and qualitative data can confirm that: (1) customers are more dependent; (2) AIP is not an add-on, but "core workflow"; and (3) government and commercial are indeed moving together. Even if a 4Q25 print comes out "bland", I believe these well-addressed points tend to reduce the perception of risk, attract less tactical investors, and reduce sensitivity to headlines.

Palantir's own management narrative in 3Q25 was around migrating new logos and pilots to operational dependency. Clients now talk about "reorganizing the company" around AIP, with multi-year expansions taking place in a few months and ACV multiplying rapidly.

On the quantitative side, this shows up in retention and backlog. In 3Q25, Net Dollar Retention (NDR) (how much existing customers spend today vs. 12 months ago) of 134%—up 600 bps QoQ—record Total Contract Value (TCV) of $2.8B, and $8.6B of remaining deal value, plus RPO of $2.6 billion growing 66% YoY.

That said, if Q4 is only solid, the market may conclude that PLTR no longer needs to surprise to sustain its valuation. Normally, a good result will generate upside; an ok result, downside. But here it could be the opposite, being rare in "AI" names. And this is the moment when PLTR's thesis starts to move away from "show me acceleration" to "show me durability".

How to Position for Palantir’s Regime Trade

Palantir's Q4 trade is more suited to a "regime trade", in my view.

If the market's reading continues to shift towards "embedded infrastructure", what changes won't be next quarter's growth but the dispersion of outcomes: the downside is no longer a collapse in the narrative but a manageable slowdown. And in this regime, valuation (even at absurd levels) can be sustained even without new surprises.

However, for this thesis to work, durability is a must for Palantir. Growth alone will not be enough. I would put the three key risks at: (1) a Q4 eventually showing more "storytelling" than evidence of enterprise-wide adoption (reorg, standardization, centralization) treating Palantir as an "AI proxy" and overcharging on multiples; (2) the lock-in thesis not showing up in the numbers that really matter if NDR/backlog lose traction, the left tail reopens; and (3) government and commercial decoupling, easily decompressing any minor volatility gained in the other two points above.

All that being said, how should investors position on PLTR to "make money" before 4Q25 in a simple way? I would put it to Palantir as two different bets, for the investor to consciously choose which one they are making:

A) Capturing the "regime shift" (durability leading to a stable multiple): the mental strategy would be not to hit the print, but to simply be exposed to the volatility compression process. This favors a base position with discipline (my current personal approach). If 4Q25 confirms durability, hold the position; if not, trim it.

B) Catching an "earnings move" (event): this is a separate trade, premised on a strong headline reaction. The problem is that, if my thesis is right, PLTR begins to lose this sensitivity, and this trade gets worse over time.

In other words, I'd say that the best setup tends to be not to hit the earnings beat, but to be on the side of the investor that management appears to be attracting: less tactical, more institutional.

Positioning Rules for Palantir Ahead of Q4

For those who already have PLTR, treat 4Q25 as a durability checkpoint, not a final decisive judgment. The required reading for Hold is primarily qualitative: enterprise-wide deployment, standardization, and expansion within the base. If this appears consistently in the earnings call, it's not time to sell. If it disappears, the trim is very plausible as PLTR becomes event-driven again.

For those who don't have PLTR and are thinking of buying, I'd say don't buy the "number" but the "evidence". A simple way would be to enter in two stages: (1) half before (so as not to miss the regime shift; (2) half after (if 4Q25 confirms what matters). This way, the risk of buying at the top of the hype is reduced.

For those thinking of going short or realizing gains, the argument for selling would not simply be "high multiples"—that much everyone knows. The argument would be to reduce PLTR if 4Q25 suggests that adoption is becoming shallow, or if the expansion/backlog signals lose quality. Then there is no volatility compression; PLTR would just be a very, very expensive AI proxy.

data.title: BBJP: The Rate Check Is A Net Negative For The Index
data.description:
The JPMorgan BetaBuilders Japan ETF (BBJP) has a heavy industrial and consumer discretionary exposure that is affected by considerations around the Yen. Japanese indices are export heavy and therefore Yen sensitive. In our previous coverage, we were concerned with the carry trade but the situation has changed a bit since then. Lower rates in the US means a less profitable carry trade on rate differentials, but there is still pressure on the Yen due to opposing fiscal and monetary policy plans by their respective stewards. However, intervention remains a way to prevent Yen shorting from going too far at least and the rate check recently has done a lot to support the Yen's value.

BBJP Breakdown

Sectoral exposures are mostly typical with a big indexation towards industrials and consumer discretionary which is going to be dominated by automotive picks like Toyota Motors (TM).

In terms of the building of the ETF, the performance is almost indistinguishable from other Japan market ETFs like the (EWJ) though with rather low costs at 0.19% against 0.49% for the EWJ, but more expensive than some UCITS options. But in general it's a very efficient ETF.

The Yen has climbed suddenly and it comes down to monetary authorities making requests for what rates they'd be buying bonds at which can be a precursor to actual intervention. This breaks down any possible carry trading that might have been going on and has boosted the Yen more than two points which is a considerable movement for the JPY - though carry trading is likely less intense in the current environment where US yields have fallen.

Nonetheless, intervention (or even just the threat of it) is helpful in unwinding some of the short bets against the Yen that are coming from the relatively incompatible fiscal and monetary policy objectives in Japan. Currently, the issue is that in a snap election, likely to consolidate the Takaichi administration's hold on Japanese politics, the expectation is for more liberal fiscal policy. At the same time, very high debt rates and the hope of the BoJ to normalise Japanese rates which have been low which would make the debt load more expensive to maintain has reduced demand for debt domestically but also in international markets which weighs on the JPY.

Bottom Line

Supposing that rate checks and actual intervention remain a tool for the BoJ to consider for a while longer, incrementally higher JPY is good for the considerable financial exposures in Japan which have significant cash flows denominated in Yen associated to domestic businesses such as retail lending and saving and domestic IB businesses as well. But other than that all the export-led exposures, which means all of the automotive exposures and quite a lot of the industrial and technology exposures, suffer when the JPY strengthens. When tariffs for the important US market are also making Japanese exports relatively more costly a stronger Yen isn't a favourable additional force.

In terms of general valuations, corporate governance and capital allocation improvements in Japanese large caps is beginning to lift valuations, which are at the upper end of historical PE ranges. The value case for the broader market isn't as strong as it used to be other than perhaps to the stretched US valuations.

data.title: TCW MetWest Total Return Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Muddling Through

Having overcome volatility and fallout from policy changes for much of the year, markets in the fourth quarter once again traversed bumpy terrain on the way to positive returns for all major asset classes. The quarter began with a government shutdown that ended up being the longest on record and deprived market participants of official data used to assess the economic backdrop, creating an environment that lacked clarity and that drove mid-quarter volatility in Treasury and equity markets. Things came into greater focus, however, with the reopening in November and subsequent flow of data that solidified a marked deterioration in the labor market and easing of inflation, prompting a second 25 basis points (bps) rate cut by the Federal Reserve (Fed) for the quarter. Meanwhile, early signs of investor skepticism regarding Al-related spending and return on investment stoked valuation and concentration fears, though realized rate cuts and stronger-than-expected GDP estimates were more than enough to support risk sentiment and guide equity markets higher. The GDP figure also caused longer dated Treasury yields to rise in December as investors repriced growth expectations for 2026, though the 50 bps of Fed easing during the quarter ultimately supported broad fixed income performance and drove a continued steepening of the curve.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter as the short and intermediate parts of the yield curve fell anywhere from 1 to 13 bps, while the more pronounced decline in yields and general tightening of spreads during the full year period resulted in a 7.3% advance- the highest annual return since 2020. Corporate credit performance for the year was similarly robust as investment grade and high yield issues returned 7.8% and 8.6%,respectively, despite a more tempered return profile for the quarter. Meanwhile, the resumption of the Fed's easing cycle and clearer direction of future Fed policy contributed to a meaningful decline in interest rate volatility that greatly benefitted agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS). The sector gained 1.7% in the fourth quarter and 8.6% for all of 2025, trailing only high yield corporates and besting comparable duration Treasuries by 171 bps on the year. The non-agency MBS sector also performed well given attractive coupons that drove carry and persistent demand in addition to resilient fundamental profiles as loan-to-value ratios continued to trend lower in aggregate. Commercial MBS (CMBS) also finished the year in firmly positive territory with private label deals outperforming agency-backed issues. An improvement in the broader commercial real estate outlook- particularly for high quality properties- also saw increased issuance and resilient performance for single asset single borrower deal types that are not represented in the Index. Finally, asset-backed securities (ABS) backed by non-traditional collateral notched positive returns for the quarter and year, along with collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), which performed well on continued investor interest.

Performance

The TCW MetWest Total Return Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) gained 1.01% (net of fees) in the fourth quarter of 2025, while the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (“Index”) returned 1.10%. For the full year, the Fund posted a gain of 7.81% (net of fees), ending approximately 50 bps ahead of the Index. Relative performance in both periods was supported by the overweight to agency MBS, which was one of the top-performing sectors over the quarter and year as interest rate volatility moderated and nominal spreads tightened meaningfully. Moreover, the broader move lower in rates saw an increase in prepayment speeds in the second half of the year that weighed on higher coupons and benefited portfolio returns given the preference for lower and belly coupons, though security selection effects in the fourth quarter were muted. Offsetting the overweight to agency MBS (and securitized sectors more broadly) was an underweight to corporate credit. Although this underweight insulated portfolio returns during the selloff in April, the position resulted in a small headwind for the full year period as spreads continued to grind tighter. Corporate issue selection was modestly negative during the quarter, though full year performance benefitted from additions made amid early year volatility that were subsequently trimmed to capture gains. Finally, the Fund's overweight duration position and emphasis on front-end yields produced a sizeable tailwind for the year as rates generally moved lower and the yield curve steepened meaningfully.

Outlook & Positioning

We head into 2026 at an inflection point as the initial headwinds from tariff and immigration overhaul appear to be fading, while pro-growth tenets of the administration's agenda are on the horizon. How the economy is impacted by the litany of policy changes over the past year is of course yet to be seen, though markets seem to be pricing in a seamless transition with credit spreads remaining near the tightest levels in recent history, equity markets setting fresh records, and measured volatility reaching 18-month lows. However, the current labor market weakness and bifurcation in consumer balance sheets suggest to us that the path forward could be bumpier than what is widely anticipated, with a possibility that the labor backdrop worsens before it gets better. Such an environment would likely hamper near-term consumer activity and dampen full-year growth potential, a scenario that appears to be discounted given broader complacency in risk asset pricing. Indeed, credit spreads at current levels provide minimal downside protection and reflect expectations for volatility to remain limited, though as the past year demonstrated, this administration has a penchant for volatility that shouldn't go underappreciated and that we expect to be a continued feature of markets.

Given the asymmetric risk and reward profile presented by corporate credit, the Fund maintains a broad underweight to the sector with an emphasis on liquidity for quick deployment into bouts of volatility. Exposure is generally more defensive in nature (up in quality and focused on non-cyclical industries) and includes select bottom-up opportunities in non-U.S. credit and below investment grade credits to capture incremental spread premium. Securitized products, meanwhile, exhibit better relative value versus corporate credit and therefore represent an overweight relative to Index allocations. A sizable position in agency MBS has been maintained given the sector's elevated spread premiums versus similar quality corporates, despite better liquidity and a government guarantee, with a bias towards lower and belly coupons that offer attractive yields and less convexity risk than higher coupons. Securitized credit positions are largely focused in off-Index areas for better collateral and yield profiles. Exposure to non-agency MBS is informed by the sector's robust collateral profiles and structural premiums that provide compelling risk-adjusted yields. Away from residential MBS, CMBS holdings are generally focused on single asset single borrower deals that allow for a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor, while ABS exposures emphasize non-traditional collateral types such as single-family rentals and high-quality CLOs. Finally, duration positioning ended the year 0.3 years long with an emphasis on the 2- and 5-Year parts of the curve given expectations for continued steepening alongside further easing by the Fed, particularly if labor markets weaken further.
